focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 16

Joel sat in the Adirondack chair Cliff had taken over at dinner the night before and watched a BCA tech come up from the dock with an evidence bag pinched away from his khakis. From deck distance, through birch trunks and sideways gold light, the whole operation had the fake calm of cleanup. Plastic bags. Clipboards. Men walking carefully because careful was what their jobs paid for. Then the tech stopped at the path, stripped off one pair of gloves, pulled on another, and took the next step without touching the same board twice, and the calm stopped looking domestic. It looked expensive. It looked like one more machine rolling toward the house.

Tuesday afternoon had gone clear and cold after a wet morning. The lake lay flat under the drop in temperature, bright enough to hurt his eyes. A fishing boat growled somewhere beyond the far trees. Every sound carried.

He had not slept. All night the house had kept making Martin noises: the refrigerator clicking on, the third stair complaining under somebody's weight, the hot-tub pump running its maintenance cycle for nobody. Joel had lain in the guest bed setting one fact against the next until a pattern started to hold. Cliff pushes Tommy on Monday. Tommy goes to the dock. Cliff dies in the water. Paul finds the will. The will points at Tommy. Martin's whole weird softness around Tommy stops reading as affection and starts reading as management. None of it was proof. All of it was pressure.

The worst part was the study, ten steps from the kitchen and twenty years from the truth, with the safe still unopened by anybody official.

Paul came out through the slider with two mugs. He had made coffee four times since dawn and had not improved at it once. He set one mug on the chair arm and sat in the chair beside J...
open_hooks_to_carry: Mallick returns: no casserole, no wrong names — she gets Martin's name right and the guests notice
The house locked down as a crime scene, forensics called
Mallick examines Cliff's body: head wound inconsistent with a fall, water in lungs indicating submersion, wrong shoes
Mallick applies her correction map from the Martin investigation to Cliff's death — who had access, motive, opportunity
Her private assessment: someone strong, desperate, and careless enough to get the shoes wrong
The contrast between this Mallick and casserole Mallick should be stark and earned

payoffs_to_land.description: The correction map Mallick built during the comic investigation — who knew which rooms, who corrected which details, who was where — now becomes the operational tool for solving Cliff's murder.

Nate's podcast recordings from draft night and subsequent evenings contain ambient audio — footsteps, voices, timing — that helps Mallick build a timeline. The recordings Nate made for content become evidence.

prev_tail_excerpt:
...urned toward the lake instead of Cliff. Even the way they were standing had started to matter.

Joel came back to them. "She's on her way. She said not to touch anything." His glance went to Paul's soaked khakis and Doug's wet right hand. "I told her we already touched things."

"I was trying to help him," Paul said, and his voice cracked on the last word. It wasn't a sob. It was the sound of a measured man's measurement giving way for a second.

"I know," Joel said.

Nate sat down on the dock next to Doug. Three feet between them, but on the dock because Doug was on the dock and that was the point. Nate's bare feet dangled over the edge, inches above the water where Cliff was floating. He realized it, muttered "shit," and pulled them back under him.

The wait settled over them in separate pieces. Paul kept the flashlight on the planks. Joel stood at the path, listening for tires. Tommy stayed where he'd stopped, too still, his hands opening and closing at his sides only when he thought nobody was looking. Doug kept seeing Cliff as he'd been in the great room two hours earlier, dry shoes on hardwood, a glass in his hand, talking about taxes and process like the future still existed.

Headlights appeared at the far end of the gravel driveway, distant at first, then the crunch of tires reached the house. Joel straightened. Paul's flashlight dropped to his side. Nate stayed seated for another second before pushing himself up.

Doug stood. His sweatpants were soaked, his knees ached from the dock, and his right hand still smelled like lake water and Cliff's skin. One last look at the water: hair drifting in the moonlight, dark shoes below the surface, the gash on the right temple a black line. Then Doug turned away and walked up the dock toward the house and the headlights.
secondary_character_beats: Each surviving guest's reaction to the new Mallick should be distinct — Joel recognizes the real detective, Tommy recognizes the danger, Nate can't stop talking, Doug goes silent, Paul tries to help
state_deltas_required: a real homicide with real suspects — Mallick without the folksy persona
Mallick is investigating a real murder; the corrections from the comedy become the evidentiary map for the tragedy
